name: band stiff leg deadlift
instructions: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and place the band around your ankles.
Hold the band with both hands in front of your thighs, palms facing your body.
Keeping your back straight and your core engaged, hinge at the hips and slowly lower your upper body towards the ground.
As you lower, push your hips back and allow your knees to bend slightly.
Lower the band towards the ground, feeling a stretch in your hamstrings.
Pause for a moment at the bottom, then engage your glutes and hamstrings to lift your upper body back up to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.

Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and place the band around your ankles. Hold the band with both hands in front of your thighs, palms facing your body. Keeping your back straight and your core engaged, hinge at the hips and slowly lower your upper body towards the ground. As you lower, push your hips back and allow your knees to bend slightly. Lower the band towards the ground, feeling a stretch in your hamstrings. Pause for a moment at the bottom, then engage your glutes and hamstrings to lift your upper body back up to the starting position. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.

conditionNotes.note: Favor symmetrical loading and supported variations if fatigue changes your posture.
Use pain-free range of motion, slower tempo, and lighter progressions when joints feel irritated.
Weight-bearing resistance exercises are beneficial for bone density when load is appropriate.
Avoid loaded spinal flexion and excessive hinge depth. Brace core and use supported positions.
Maintain consistent breathing throughout. Avoid breath-holding.
Both aerobic and resistance training improve insulin sensitivity. Monitor blood sugar before, during, and after sessions. Have a fast-acting carb available.
Rhythm-based exercises and large-amplitude movements are beneficial. Focus on motor confidence.
Focus on muscle co-contraction and joint stability. Avoid locking out joints passively.
Functional patterns are generally safe. Prioritize pelvic floor awareness and avoid Valsalva.
Start light with higher reps and shorter ROM. Progress load gradually as pain-free capacity increases.
